rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient is acutely and systemically unwell (fever, severe diffuse myalgia, profound weakness), lives alone with a high falls risk, and telehealth cannot examine her or measure vitals to exclude a serious infection. That combination warrants urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3) — examination, observations and bloods — plus immediate cessation of the offending drug. It is NOT a life-threatening emergency: the underlying process is a mild, transient drug reaction and (on the sealed clinician observations) she is haemodynamically stable, so a 000 ambulance / ED sepsis activation (T4–T5) would be over-triage. Equally it is not safe to leave to routine GP or self-care (T0–T1), because a febrile, functionally-declined older adult who cannot be examined remotely could harbour an infection and could fall at home.

escalation_edges.rationale: New pleuritic chest pain or breathlessness could indicate hydralazine-DILE serositis (pleuritis/pericarditis) or a separate cardiorespiratory emergency, needing emergency department assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get chest pain or find it hard to breathe, go to the nearest emergency department straight away — or call 000 if it is severe or you feel faint with it.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever spiking above 39 °C, shaking chills/rigors, or feeling much worse
escalation_edges.rationale: A high spiking fever or rigors shifts probability back toward serious infection and warrants emergency assessment rather than waiting for the planned review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your temperature climbs above 39 °C, you get shaking chills, or you feel a lot worse, don't wait for the appointment — go to the emergency department or call for help.

escalation_edges.rationale: A spreading or blistering rash may indicate a severe cutaneous drug reaction or vasculitis and needs urgent emergency evaluation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If a rash appears and spreads quickly, or your skin starts to blister, go to the emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Dizziness/lightheadedness on standing, a fall, or weakness so severe she cannot safely move about at home
escalation_edges.rationale: Orthostatic symptoms or worsening weakness in a person living alone raise the falls risk and may signal deterioration; bring the assessment forward and arrange support.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel dizzy standing up, have a fall, or get too weak to move around safely on your own, call for urgent help today — and phone someone to be with you.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, unable to get up after a fall, or unable to summon help
escalation_edges.rationale: A collapse or an unwitnessed fall with a long lie in a person living alone is an emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you collapse or fall and can't get up, call 000 for an ambulance — keep your phone on you at all times today.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is acutely weak with a high falls risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises the disposition toward same-day in-person assessment and mandates arranging someone to check in; a phone must stay within reach and walkways kept clear.
Lower threshold to assess in person and to exclude infection; more conservative safety-netting for deterioration and falls.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Hypertension with the antihypertensive being stopped
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Blood pressure must not be left unmanaged — the urgent review must arrange a replacement agent; NSAID use should be minimised given BP and renal considerations.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Stop the hydralazine now and arrange to be seen in person today — through your GP, a same-day clinic, or urgent care — for an examination, observations and blood tests to make sure this isn't an infection and to confirm the cause. Take the anti-inflammatory with food for the muscle pain, and rest. Keep your phone with you and ask someone to check on you.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Phone your GP or a same-day/urgent-care clinic today for an in-person appointment; if you cannot be seen today, attend an urgent care centre.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, call the after-hours GP/home-doctor service or healthdirect on 1800 022 222 for advice; use the emergency department for any of the red-flag symptoms above.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the clinician that hydralazine was started about 8 weeks ago and that a drug-induced lupus reaction is suspected; ask them to document the adverse drug reaction, check observations and bloods (FBC, CRP/ESR, renal function, urinalysis, ANA and anti-histone antibodies), and arrange a replacement antihypertensive.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as self-care or routine review — advising rest at home for 'a virus' — leaves a febrile, systemically unwell older adult who lives alone unexamined, missing possible infection and the falls risk.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving infection progresses unrecognised, or the patient falls at home with a prolonged lie and no help.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: A 24–48 hour GP appointment without same-day in-person assessment is borderline acceptable only if drug cessation and firm safety-netting are given, but it delays exclusion of infection in an unexaminable, unwell patient living alone.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Activating a 000 ambulance or an ED sepsis pathway with IV antibiotics for a haemodynamically stable patient with no septic focus — the over-escalation this case is designed to catch.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot exclude a serious infection (no examination, no observations, no bloods), and the drug-induced lupus diagnosis is presumptive pending serology.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Hold both hypotheses: act on the strong drug-reaction clue by stopping hydralazine, and manage the residual infection risk with urgent same-day in-person assessment plus explicit red-flag safety-netting — neither dismiss it as a virus nor over-escalate to an ambulance.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Collapsing the uncertainty prematurely in either direction — declaring it definitely a drug reaction and advising home rest, or declaring it sepsis and dispatching an ambulance for IV antibiotics.
